spec_match.rationale: Despite the framing the demo does NOT actually use ::part() selectors to apply the state visuals. activeStates drives inline JS-set style attributes on a plain <span>, not host-page CSS rules against a shadow component. The 'live component preview' is theatre, not a real ::part() interaction.

interactive.rationale: Buttons toggle states and the preview updates, but the underlying mechanism is JS inline styles. Calling it a 'state machine' for ::part() is misleading; a visitor who inspects the DOM sees ordinary div styling, not ::part() chains in action.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises 'see the CSS rules activate in real time' — the rule-display panel does update text, but the rules themselves are not applied; the visual change comes from JS-set inline styles. Misalignment between framing and mechanism.

edge_cases.rationale: No probe of what's still rejected (combinators, :has, tree-structural pseudos). The 'before/after' claims in feature-cards are exaggerated ('impossible to style placeholder from outside shadow' — actually ::placeholder has been styleable inside the shadow long before; the change is specifically about chaining after ::part).

references.rationale: See-also section links to sibling concepts only; the direct https://chromestatus.com/feature/5195333643272192 link is absent. Rule #3 violated.

design_system.rationale: Uses --accent-amber which exists, but feature-cards rely on inline color values via inline styles for the partLabel/partButton. The probe uses CSS.supports-equivalent insertRule which is a fine pattern but feature-list cards are static information cards (acceptable as supporting context but the main interactive surface is the inline-style theatre).

openQuestions.title: Replace inline-style theatre with real ::part() rules
openQuestions.detail: The state-toggling should append/remove host-page CSS rules of the form `state-machine[data-states~='hover']::part(button):hover { ... }` (or just `::part(button)[state~='hover']`) so a visitor inspecting elements sees an actual shadow-DOM custom element and the new chains in action. Right now the visible behaviour proves nothing about ::part().

openQuestions.title: Add the chromestatus reference link
openQuestions.detail: Rule #3 requires every concept page to carry the chromestatus.com/feature/<id> link. The see-also only links to siblings.

openQuestions.title: Fix the misleading 'before/after' claims
openQuestions.detail: The feature-cards say things like 'Before: impossible to style placeholder from outside shadow' — but ::placeholder styling inside shadow was always allowed; the change is about chaining ::placeholder after ::part. Tighten the language so it reflects what the spec actually unblocks.

openQuestions.title: Probe the rejection envelope
openQuestions.detail: Add a panel that runs CSS.supports() against a fixed list of still-forbidden chains (combinators, :has, ::part chained after ::part) and marks them rejected. Right now the demo only celebrates the new acceptances.

summary: The framing oversells what the page actually does. Visually it looks like a state-machine demo of ::part() chaining, but underneath it's inline-style JS theatre on a plain element with no shadow root. Needs a real ::part rebuild plus the missing chromestatus link.
